description: Gomacs - Minimal Emacs-like CLI editor written in pure Go with tcell for terminal handling

userStories.title: Project Setup and Terminal Initialization
userStories.description: As a developer, I need the Go project scaffolding and terminal initialization so that the editor can take over the terminal screen.
acceptanceCriteria: go.mod initialized with module name gomacs
tcell added as dependency (github.com/gdamore/tcell/v2)
Main function initializes a tcell.Screen, enters raw mode, and cleans up on exit
Running go run . shows a blank terminal screen and exits cleanly on C-c
go build produces a working binary
Typecheck passes

userStories.title: Buffer Data Structure
userStories.description: As a developer, I need an in-memory buffer data structure to hold text content so that editing operations can be performed efficiently.
acceptanceCriteria: Buffer struct stores lines as a slice of rune slices ([][]rune)
Buffer tracks cursor position (row, column)
Buffer supports inserting a character at cursor position
Buffer supports deleting a character at cursor position (backspace)
Buffer supports inserting a newline (splitting a line)
Buffer supports deleting at beginning of line (joining with previous line)
Unit tests pass for all buffer operations
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Use [][]rune for lines. Create buffer.go and buffer_test.go.

userStories.title: File Open and Display
userStories.description: As a user, I want to open a file from the command line so that I can view its contents in the editor.
acceptanceCriteria: Running gomacs filename.txt opens and displays the file content
Running gomacs with no arguments opens an empty buffer
File content is rendered correctly in the terminal
Long lines are truncated at screen width (no wrapping)
Lines beyond the screen height are not displayed (scrolling comes later)
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Integrate buffer with tcell screen rendering. Read file via os.ReadFile, split by newline.

userStories.title: Basic Cursor Movement
userStories.description: As a user, I want to move the cursor using Emacs keybindings so that I can navigate within the file.
acceptanceCriteria: C-f moves cursor forward one character
C-b moves cursor backward one character
C-n moves cursor down one line
C-p moves cursor up one line
C-a moves cursor to beginning of line
C-e moves cursor to end of line
Arrow keys also work for movement
Cursor does not move beyond buffer boundaries
Cursor is visually displayed at the correct position
Typecheck passes

userStories.title: Scrolling and Viewport
userStories.description: As a user, I want the view to scroll when the cursor moves beyond the visible area so that I can edit files larger than the screen.
acceptanceCriteria: View scrolls down when cursor moves below the last visible line
View scrolls up when cursor moves above the first visible line
M-v (Alt+V or Esc then V) scrolls up one page
C-v scrolls down one page
M-< moves to beginning of buffer
M-> moves to end of buffer
Scroll offset is tracked and rendering adjusts accordingly
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Track scrollOffset (top visible line). Adjust on cursor movement and page scroll commands.

userStories.title: Text Insertion and Basic Editing
userStories.description: As a user, I want to type characters and perform basic edits so that I can modify the file content.
acceptanceCriteria: Printable characters are inserted at cursor position
Enter inserts a new line and moves cursor to the beginning of the next line
Backspace deletes the character before the cursor
C-d deletes the character at the cursor (forward delete)
C-d at end of line joins the next line to the current line
Backspace at beginning of line joins the current line to the previous line
Screen re-renders correctly after each edit
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Use buffer methods from US-002. Mark buffer as modified on any edit.

userStories.title: Status Line
userStories.description: As a user, I want to see a status line at the bottom of the screen so that I know which file I'm editing and where my cursor is.
acceptanceCriteria: Status line is displayed on the second-to-last row of the terminal
Status line shows the filename (or [No Name] for new buffers)
Status line shows [Modified] when the buffer has unsaved changes
Status line shows cursor position as Line X, Col Y
Status line has a visually distinct background (reverse video)
Status line updates on every cursor move or edit
Typecheck passes

userStories.title: File Save with C-x C-s
userStories.description: As a user, I want to save the file so that my changes are persisted to disk.
acceptanceCriteria: C-x C-s saves the current buffer to the file it was opened from
After saving, the [Modified] indicator is cleared
A Saved message is briefly shown in the status line or message area
If buffer has no filename, saving is skipped with an informational message
File is written safely (write to temp then rename, or equivalent)
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Requires C-x prefix key state machine. Implement prefix key handling here.

userStories.title: Quit with C-x C-c
userStories.description: As a user, I want to quit the editor cleanly so that the terminal is restored to its normal state.
acceptanceCriteria: C-x C-c quits the editor
Terminal is fully restored on exit (cursor visible, normal mode)
If buffer is modified, a warning message is shown and quit is cancelled
Repeating C-x C-c when modified forces quit without saving
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Reuses C-x prefix handling from US-008.

userStories.title: Kill and Yank (Cut and Paste)
userStories.description: As a user, I want to cut and paste text using Emacs kill/yank so that I can move text around efficiently.
acceptanceCriteria: C-k kills (cuts) text from cursor to end of line into the kill ring
C-k on an empty line kills the newline (joins with next line)
Consecutive C-k commands append to the kill ring entry
C-y yanks (pastes) the last killed text at cursor position
Yanked text is inserted correctly, handling multi-line content
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Implement a simple kill ring (can be a single string or slice of strings).

userStories.title: Mark and Region
userStories.description: As a user, I want to select a region of text using mark so that I can kill or copy specific sections.
acceptanceCriteria: C-SPC (Ctrl+Space) sets the mark at the current cursor position
The region between mark and cursor (point) is visually highlighted
C-w kills (cuts) the region and stores it in the kill ring
M-w (Alt+W) copies the region to the kill ring without deleting
C-g deactivates the mark (cancels selection)
After kill or copy, the mark is deactivated
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Requires determining region start/end from mark and point positions.

userStories.title: Undo
userStories.description: As a user, I want to undo changes so that I can recover from mistakes.
acceptanceCriteria: C-/ undoes the last editing operation
C-_ also triggers undo (alternative binding)
Undo supports multiple levels (at least 100 undo steps)
Undo restores both content and cursor position
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Store undo entries as snapshots of affected lines + cursor position. Keep it simple — full buffer snapshots are fine for small files.

userStories.title: Incremental Search
userStories.description: As a user, I want to search for text incrementally so that I can quickly find content in the file.
acceptanceCriteria: C-s starts incremental search forward
Characters typed during search update the search query and jump to the next match
C-s again during search jumps to the next match
C-r starts incremental search backward
Enter or C-g exits search mode (C-g restores original cursor position)
The current match is visually highlighted
The search query is shown in the status line or a message area
Typecheck passes
userStories.notes: Requires a search mode in the event loop state machine. Temporarily override key handling while in search mode.
